coredata.dc:title: A high-performance color conversion and UV to blue light blocking materials enabled by iron-coordinated cellulose

coredata.dc:description: Color conversion, long-wavelength light emission by absorbing short wavelength light using color conversion materials, is not only the basis for successful operation of many contemporary display and lighting technologies, but also the most effective strategy for shielding harmful UV and blue light. However, high cost and complicated processing inevitably hinder the development of color conversion materials. Inspired by FeCl3 solution, color-conversion cellulose films with low haze and high transparency were successfully prepared by the coordination and stacking of Fe3+. For the first time, it was found that when the coordinated Fe3+ forms a stacked layer, the film blocks the entire UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL) region (400–450 nm), and the absorbed UV and HEBL is converted into low energy (long wavelength) light in the yellow and green regions. The films also demonstrate efficient blue light conversion and allows other light in the visible region to enter smoothly. The light conversion performance of the films is substantiated through their application on blue-, green-, and red-LEDs. By means of light absorption and conversion, the films are capable of efficiently screening blue light emitted by WLEDs in lighting systems, video game console screen, computer screen, and mobile phone screen, as well as UV radiation from the sun. Encouragingly, the incorporation of Fe3+ enables films to achieve an exceptional flame-retardant feature. These films as color conversion materials will have wide applicability in eye protection and flexible photoelectric devices due to their exceptional combination performance and are highly suitable for large-scale continuous production.

head.ce:title: A high-performance color conversion and UV to blue light blocking materials enabled by iron-coordinated cellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Hemp and Product in Cold Region of Ministry of Education, School of Light Industry and Textile, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Color conversion, long-wavelength light emission by absorbing short wavelength light using color conversion materials, is not only the basis for successful operation of many contemporary display and lighting technologies, but also the most effective strategy for shielding harmful UV and blue light. However, high cost and complicated processing inevitably hinder the development of color conversion materials. Inspired by FeCl3 solution, color-conversion cellulose films with low haze and high transparency were successfully prepared by the coordination and stacking of Fe3+. For the first time, it was found that when the coordinated Fe3+ forms a stacked layer, the film blocks the entire UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL) region (400–450 nm), and the absorbed UV and HEBL is converted into low energy (long wavelength) light in the yellow and green regions. The films also demonstrate efficient blue light conversion and allows other light in the visible region to enter smoothly. The light conversion performance of the films is substantiated through their application on blue-, green-, and red-LEDs. By means of light absorption and conversion, the films are capable of efficiently screening blue light emitted by WLEDs in lighting systems, video game console screen, computer screen, and mobile phone screen, as well as UV radiation from the sun. Encouragingly, the incorporation of Fe3+ enables films to achieve an exceptional flame-retardant feature. These films as color conversion materials will have wide applicability in eye protection and flexible photoelectric devices due to their exceptional combination performance and are highly suitable for large-scale continuous production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Light is fundamental to our existence. Light not only generates the diverse colors of the world but also confers benefits to all living organisms [1]. Appropriate exposure to sunlight is advantageous to human health. Recently, the advent of advanced lighting technologies has heightened human exposure to artificial light sources, such as flat panel TVs, smartphones, and tablets incorporating backlight technology, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), and compact fluorescent lamps [2–4]. This light, defined by wavelengths ranging from 400 to 500 nm, occupies the blue light region of the visible spectrum [5–7]. However, mounting evidence indicates that exposure to there artificial lights pose a hazard to human eyes [8]. More critically, an increasing number of people are being exposed to harmful blue light, especially during night-time use of smartphones and the long-term use of computer equipment by all researchers. Blue light exerts deleterious effects on the eyes, such as optic nerve damage, photoreceptor cell destruction, and cumulative eye strain [9,10]. In the current information age, the pervasive use of electronic devices highlights the necessity for the design and synthesis of novel HEBL resistant films to meet these demands. It is desirable to develop materials to filter most of the light within the 400–450 nm range and a small amount of light within the 450–500 nm range, which can strike a balance between visual damage of shorter blue light and beneficial non-visual functions of longer blue light, thereby effectively reducing the harms of blue light while offering a comfortable visual experience.

Typically, blue light can be attenuated using either absorptive or dichroic filter [11,12]. The attenuation of light occurs due to the absorption properties of the glass substrate employed in the absorptive filter. To put it differently, the optical filter does not reflect the blocked light; rather, it absorbs and retains it within the filter [2]. A key advantage of this filter is its insensitivity to incident angles. The filter consistently preserves its absorption and transmission properties, even under conditions of wide-angle incidence. Conversely, a dichroic filter reflects undesirable wavelengths while allowing the transmission of necessary spectral components. This effect is accomplished through the exploitation of interference properties of one or more additional light waves generated by material layers with varying refractive indices. Unlike absorptive filters, this technique suffers from the limitation of angle sensitivity, making it infeasible to screen the desired wavelength unless observed at the specified angle. Considering these factors, we concentrated on absorbing undesirable wavelengths (UV to blue light region) and converting them into longer wavelengths to reduce light loss during the shielding of unwanted wavelengths.

Color conversion materials can absorb short-wavelength light and emit long-wavelength light, which can effectively shield harmful UV rays and HEBL. Luminescent materials characterized by light absorption and conversion abilities typically encompass organic dyes [13], semiconductor quantum dots [14–16], and carbon dots [17,18]. Organic dyes are significantly hindered by aggregation, leading to emission quenching and inferior photochemical stability, while semiconductor quantum dots, containing cadmium, are restricted by toxicity. Conversely, fluorescent carbon dots are readily synthesized, sustainable, and possess adjustable optical properties [19,20]. However, aggregation of carbon dots can cause fluorescence quenching. Meanwhile, the large-scale production of carbon dots is also limited. Metal ions are pervasive in nature and are pivotal in the formation of functional materials across disciplines such as materials science, biology, and chemistry [21]. Hu's group [22] changed the crystal structure of cellulose by coordinating Cu2+ with one-dimensional cellulose nanofibers for the first time. As a result, the spacing between polymer chains was enlarged into molecular channels for Li+ intercalation and rapid transport, thus realizing the rapid transport of Li+ along the polymer chain. The rare earth metal cations are often used in fluorescent materials or color conversion materials [23,24]. Nonetheless, the utilization of rare earth metals is constrained by their high cost. Fe3+, a type of transition metal ion, is extensively employed as a dopant due to its ionic radius (0.64 Å) being comparable to that of Ti4+ (0.68 Å), facilitating the cation substitution process. The Fe3+ absorption takes place in the near-UV region of the electromagnetic spectrum, making it frequently utilized as a UV absorber [25,26]. Khalifa et al. [27]investigated the atypical photoluminescence properties of Fe3+-doped polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films. The influence of Fe3+ doping on the photoluminescence properties of PVA was examined over a temperature range of 10 to 300 K. Although Fe3+ has been used in many fields, it has never been found as a color conversion material, especially for application in harmful blue light blocking and conversion.

In this work, we found for the first time that FeCl3 solution can effectively shield UV and HEBL while possessing powerful color (wavelength) conversion capability. Inspired by FeCl3 solution, the fabrication of flame retardant, UV to HEBL-blocking and color convertible Fe3+-coordinated cellulose films using a simple and eco-friendly strategy was explored. We applied the cellulose film as color conversion material to emit yellow and green lights excited by UV and blue LEDs, which result in high color conversion performance. By means of light absorption and conversion, the films can effectively obstruct blue light emitted by WLEDs in lighting systems, video game console screen, mobile phone screen, and computer screen, as well as UV light emitted by sun. The color conversion material prepared using Fe3+ will offer broad applicability in blue light blocking due to its low-cost and exceptional combination performance relative to traditional color conversion materials. The film is eminently suitable for large-scale continuous production and adequately meets the demands of various applications. Our findings offer a novel alternative to conventional anti-blue light films, effectively mitigating retinal damage. Additionally, it furnishes foundational support for development next-generation lighting and display devices equipped with blue light screening capabilities in future applications.

Cotton-derived circular qualitative filter papers were provided by Hangzhou Special Paper Industry Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), glycerol, and sodium chloroacetate were provided by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). The ionic liquid 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl) and iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd.

In order to realize the coordination and immobilization of Fe3+ with cellulose, cellulose fibers were modified by a classical carboxymethylation process. Cellulose powders (20 g) ground by filter paper were submerged in a 500 mL 1 M ClCH2COONa solution prepared in 15 % (w/v) NaOH and stirred at 50 °C for 2 h. Then, the fibers were isolated through filtration and subjected to triple rinsing with tap water to eliminate residual impurities. The recovered fibers underwent an additional rinsing with deionized water, followed by drying at 60 °C. The methylene blue assay was utilized to determine the carboxyl group content present in both the unmodified and modified fibers [28].

Cellulose hydrogel was used as substrate to prepare functional films. Initially, cellulose powder and AmimCl were introduced into a round-bottomed flask. The cellulose/AmimCl mixture, with a cellulose mass fraction of 2 wt%, was mechanically stirred at the designated temperature. The cellulose solution is then degassed in a vacuum oven to remove the bubbles and then treated at 90 °C to reduce solution viscosity. The resulting solution was cast onto silicon wafers with a VTC100 Vacuum Spin Coater and subsequently immersed in distilled water. To remove residual AmimCl from the regenerated cellulose, these gels (1.2 mm thickness) were washed using deionized water at least three times until no Cl– ions were detected by the AgNO3 test.

Cellulose hydrogels containing a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl group sites were immersed in different concentrations of FeCl3 solution (0.15 M, 0.25 M, 0.35 M) for 2 h. To reduce the brittleness of the film and increase its flexibility, glycerol (5 wt%) was added and continued to soak for 10 min. The obtained hydrogel was washed with deionized water. Subsequently, the Fe3+ adsorbed hydrogels were affixed to a glass plate to prevent shrinkage and dried. Carboxymethylated cellulose-Fe3+ films with a thickness of approximately 0.12 mm were produced, denoted as CCF-Fe3+1, CCF-Fe3+2, and CCF-Fe3+3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of UV and blue light screening and conversion performance
ce:section.ce:para: The UV and blue light blocking capabilities of CCF-Fe3+ films were assessed using UV–vis spectrophotometry. The UV and blue light screening efficacy of CCF-Fe3+ films was evaluated based on optical transmittance. The UV and blue light protection percentages of the CCF-Fe3+ films were computed eq 1–4.

The UV and blue light shielding and conversion capacities of FeCl3 solution and cellulose-Fe3+ films were also evaluated using UV LED (9 W, 350 nm) and blue LED (9 W, 450 nm). FeCl3 solution and cellulose-Fe3+ films were placed on the UV LED and blue LED to obtain photographs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The practical applications of cellulose-Fe3+ films
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of the fabricated cellulose-Fe3+ films in practical applications was validated through their ability to filter emitted from lighting systems, game console screen, mobile phone screen, and computer screen. The measurements were recorded with CCF and CCF-Fe3+ positioned between light sources and OHSP350 spectrometer.

The microcombustion calorimetry (MCC) assessment was conducted using a microcalorimeter MCC-2 (FTT, UK) following the ASTM D7309-2007 standard. The LOI test was performed utilizing a FTT0077 oxygen index meter. The dimensions of the samples were 80 mm × 10 mm.

XRD (D/max2200VPC), ATR-FTIR spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc), and XPS (ESCALAB 250Xi) were used to analyze the crystal and chemical structures of films. SEM (Hitachi S-4800, Japan) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were used to observe the morphology and elemental distribution of films. TGA (STA449 F3) was used to test the thermal stability of films under an N2 atmosphere from 30 °C to 800 °C. UTM2203 universal testing machine was used to measure the tensile stress–strain curves. UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950, China) was used to test the optical performance of films. OHSP350 spectrometer was used to test the electronic product parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed process conceptfrom FeCl3 solution to color conversion films
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 that harmful blue light can be effectively shielded only at high concentration or large solution volume, suggesting that the efficiency of the FeCl3 solution in shielding blue light is related to the number iron ions in the direction of direct light, that is, the concentration of iron ions. A detailed schematic diagram of how FeCl3 solution shields UV and blue light is shown in Fig. 1a. In the absence of FeCl3 solution, the device displayed blue light only. Then, the blue LED was utilize as a light source to excite the FeCl3 solution. When the light irradiates FeCl3 solution from the bottom, the light does not hit Fe3+ before leaving the solution, the light will directly transmit through FeCl3 solution and the propagation direction will not change. When the light hits Fe3+, the light is partially absorbed by Fe3+ and participates in the color conversion process of Fe3+, partially not absorbed, and the unabsorbed light is continues to propagate in the FeCl3 solution in the original direction of propagation. Part of the blue light is first absorbed by the bottom Fe3+, and then absorbed by the upper Fe3+. Since the spatial distribution of Fe3+ in water is random, the blue light is absorbed layer by layer. When the concentration of FeCl3 solution is low or the volume is small, the number of Fe3+ is small and the distribution in space is relatively dispersed, and the limited Fe3+ can only absorb a small amount of light. Short-wave blue light has relatively high energy light, stronger than UV light, blue light is partially absorbed by Fe3+, unabsorbed blue light then encounters Fe3+ and is absorbed and converted again, several times after being completely absorbed and converted, while UV light can be completely absorbed and converted by iron ions. Based on this, we tried to inject FeCl3 solution into our designed container (cellulose matrix) as a blue light filter.

The fabrication process of multifunctional cellulose-based light conversion film (CCF-Fe3+) is shown in Fig. 3 . Based on the analysis in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2, the idea was to introduce Fe3+ into the cellulose matrix to form an ionic layer for efficient blue light shielding and color conversion. A simple and green method was used to prepare cellulose hydrogel based on the efficient dissolution of cellulose in ionic liquids. Then color-convertible cellulose-based transparent films with high transmittance and low haze were obtained by coordination with Fe3+. As is shown in Fig. 3, to enhance the transmittance and decrease the haze of cellulose-based light conversion film, as well as to strengthen the bonding interaction between Fe3+ and cellulose, carboxymethylated cellulose fibers were fabricated through the etherification reaction of sodium chloroacetate and cellulose. Subsequently, carboxymethylated cellulose fibers are dissolved in AmimCl. After that, cellulose solution with high optical transparency was obtained. The cellulose solution was then cast onto silicon wafers to obtain a thickness of about 1.2 mm, then soaked in the distilled water, yielding a transparent cellulose hydrogel. Then, the hydrogel films were dipped into different concentrations of FeCl3 solution. Significantly, apart from the raw materials and solvents (AmimCl and water), no additional chemicals are employed in the preparation process, and all solvents can be recycled and reused. Concurrently, such a straightforward and eco-friendly manufacturing approach is fully compatible with large-scale continuous production lines (roll-to-roll) for multifunctional cellulose films, which is pivotal for transitioning cellulose-based light conversion films from the laboratory to practical, real-world applications in the future. The light conversion films were obtained by drying of Fe3+-coordinated cellulose matrix. During the drying process, the structure of Fe3+-coordinated cellulose matrix undergoes densification, which leads to the stacking of Fe3+ in the cellulose matrix and thus the formation of a metal ion layer. According to the previous analysis, Fe3+ can effectively shield and and convert blue light when they form an ionic layer in the cellulose matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color conversion performance of CCF-Fe3+ films
ce:section.ce:para: A proof-of-concept demonstration was conducted to validate the feasibility of employing CCF-Fe3+ films as color-converting materials in LEDs. Fig. 4 a illustrates the Schematic of the experimental setup, where a piece of color-converting film is located above a UV or blue LED lamp. As is shown in Fig. 4b, neither glass nor CCF can block UV light or convert it. When the CCF-Fe3+ films are used to screen the UV LED, the UV light can be completely shielded, and at the same time the UV light is converted to yellow light, and it can be observed that the intensity of the yellow light emitted is similar. Similarly, neither glass nor CCF can block UV light or convert it. When the CCF-Fe3+ films are used to block the blue LED, blue light can be completely shielded and converted into green light, and the emitted green light intensity can be observed to gradually increases. It can also be observed from Fig. 4b that blue light cannot be blocked when the blue LED is blocked with cellulose hydrogel-Fe3+. Similar to metal ions in solution, Fe3+ may not be effective in shielding against blue light because it is more dispersed in the hydrogel. This is due to the large gap between the Fe3+ distributed in the hydrogel, which can only absorb a small amount of blue light, while most of the blue light passes through the gap between the Fe3+, or through the hydrogel by refraction, etc., and therefore cannot effectively shield the blue light. Also because only a small amount of blue light is absorbed, less green light is converted than the amount of blue light that passes through the hydrogel and therefore the green light cannot be observed by the naked eye, only the blue light. During the drying process, as the water is removed, the structure of the cellulose hydrogel transforms and becomes more dense, and the spatially random distribution of Fe3+ in the cellulose hydrogel gradually forms an ionic layer with the stack of cellulose. The mechanism of blocking harmful blue light by the ion layer is shown in Fig. 1a. It can be observed that during the drying process, the thickness of the hydrogel gradually decreases, Fe3+ gradually builds up and the distance between Fe3+ gradually decreases, so that when light passes through, most of the light can be absorbed by Fe3+ and converted to green light visible to the naked eye, and only a small amount of blue light passes through, even when the amount of Fe3+ is high enough, the blue light can be completely absorbed and converted. Video S2 demonstrates the light shielding and conversion properties of CCF-Fe3+ films at different Fe3+ concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and blue light screening properties of CCF-Fe3+ films
ce:section.ce:para: The optical transparency and UV-screening performance of materials are highly advantageous for wearable and optical devices. Excessive exposure to UV radiation can hasten the degradation of organic substances. Therefore, the development of transparent films with anti-UV function is essential for various applications. The blue light filtering capability of the material aids in mitigating eye damage induced by electronics. To investigate the efficacy of cellulose-based films in filtering UV and blue light, the transmittance spectra of CCF and Fe3+-coordinated cellulose films were measured. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, CCF exhibits high transparency in both the UV-A and visible region (90 % at 350 nm and 93.3 % at 550 nm). CCF shields only 21.5 % of UVB, 9.1 % of UVA, 7.9 % of HEBL and 7.8 % of other blue light, suggesting that CCF is inadequate for screening UV-A, UV-B, and blue light. As displayed in Fig. 5a, all CCF-Fe3+ films have high transparency. The incorporation of varying contents of Fe3+ into cellulose films enables them to absorb UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL. Fig. 5b and 5c illustrate the transmittance at 550 nm of CCF-Fe3+ films in relation to their UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL screening efficiency. In all CCF-Fe3+ films, the transmission of UV-A and UV-B is completely inhibited, while maintaining high visible light transparency. Specifically, CCF-Fe3+1 can shield 100 % of UVB, 100 % of UVA, 99.9 % of HEBL and 71.1 % of other blue light (Fig. 5d). CCF-Fe3+1 exhibits a light transmittance value reaching 88.3 % at 550 nm. The UV and blue light screening ratio of CCF-Fe3+ films was further augmented with increasing Fe3+ concentration. CCF-Fe3+2 can blocked 100 % of UVB, 100 % of UVA, 99.9 % of HEBL and 87.6 % of other blue light. The transmittance of CCF-Fe3+2 is still as high as 84.5 % at 550 nm. The blue light screening efficacy of CCF-Fe3+ films increased significantly with increasing Fe3+ concentration. CCF-Fe3+3 can shield 100 % of UVB, 100 % of UVA, 100 % of of HEBL and 97.6 % of other blue light. As can be seen from Fig. 5a, CCF-Fe3+ films exhibit sufficient visible light transparency, and all CCF-Fe3+ films can completely shield UV light, which is consistent with Fig. 4. In addition, UV light is completely shielded while being converted to yellow light, and the shielding efficiency reaches 100 %. It can be observed that the intensity of yellow light in Fig. 4 is similar, indicating that the conversion efficiency of three films to UV light is similar. Meanwhile, CCF-Fe3+ films all showed excellent HEBL shielding performance, with shielding ratios above 99 %. With the increase of Fe3+ concentration, the shielding region continued to extend to other blue light regions at 450–500 nm, with shielding rates of 71.1 %, 87.6 % and 97.6 %, respectively, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 4, and all the films can block blue light. Unlike the conversion efficiency similar to UV light, the conversion efficiency of CCF-Fe3+ films for blue light increases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, which can be verified by the gradual enhancement of green light intensity in Fig. 4. The light blocking ratios of CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films were calculated based on the emission spectral intensities when screening 395 nm and 450 nm LED chips. CCF exhibits negligible screening effect against 395 nm and 460 nm wavelengths. The UV light blocking ratios of the glass, CCF, and CCF-Fe3+ films are 10.1 % (glass), 13.2 % (CCF), and 100 % (CCF-Fe3+1, CCF-Fe3+2, and CCF-Fe3+3), respectively, as displayed in Fig. 5e. The blue light shielding ratios of the glass, CCF, and CCF-Fe3+ films are 2.3 % (glass), 8.1 % (CCF), 100 % (CCF-Fe3+1, CCF-Fe3+2, and CCF-Fe3+3), respectively, as displayed in Fig. 5f. Fig. 5g exhibits the digital images of CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films. The integration of Fe3+ ions into the cellulose matrix imparts a yellow hue to the CCF-Fe3+ films, with the coloration progressively intensifying as the Fe3+ concentration increases. When viewed through the CCF-Fe3+ films, the text “Cellulose Film” remained largely unaffected by the intrinsic coloration of the films. The existence of CCF-Fe3+ film covers on computer and mobile phone screens did not compromise the readability of the screen images (Fig. S2). The superior UV-blocking capability of CCF-Fe3+ films was further evidenced through fluorescence pattern analysis. The anti-counterfeit pattern displayed distinct fluorescence emission under UV light (365 nm) when CCF was utilized. Conversely, the fluorescence emission of the anti-counterfeit pattern was unobservable due to the UV light being blocked by the CCF-Fe3+ films. In summary, CCF-Fe3+ films concurrently exhibited high transmittance alongside effective UV blocking properties.

The stability of light conversion material is of paramount importance, as light conversion materials must endure extensive periods of continuous use. This research investigated the impact of UV irradiation and thermal treatment on the stability of light screening materials. The CCF-Fe3+3 film was subjected to UV irradiation utilizing a xenon lamp or thermal treatment at 90 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the resultant films were evaluated using UV–vis light transmittance, as depicted in Fig. 5i. Notably, the UV and blue light screening capacity of CCF-Fe3+3 remained stable in the UV and blue light transmittance regions, indicating the ability of film to endure prolonged UV exposure. Intriguingly, following thermal treatment, the light shielding efficacy of CCF-Fe3+3 was enhanced and transmittance reduced, suggesting that thermal processing augments light shielding performance. This exceptional light screening performance was ascribed to the discoloration of CCF-Fe3+ films induced by heating [25].

The blocking efficacy of HEBL and transmittance to visible light of CCF-Fe3+ film was evaluated in comparison to previously documented light blocking films (Fig. 5j) [6,29–44]. The ideal overall performance trend is represented by a diagonal arrow. The CCF-Fe3+ film, particularly CCF-Fe3+1, exhibited superior performance in screening HEBL at 450 nm, achieving 99.9 % efficiency. Furthermore, it demonstrated exceptional transparency, with a measurement of 88.3 % at 550 nm. However, several works have documented moderate HEBL-screening efficiency and transparency levels. Several investigations have shown a high HEBL-screening efficacy of approximately 90 %; however, they are limited by insufficient transmittance. Other studies have shown a high degree of transmittance (approximately 80 %), yet they were ineffective in screening HEBL. Consequently, our CCF-Fe3+ film, which provides numerous benefits such as highly efficient UV and HEBL screening, excellent transparency, exceptional photostability and thermal stability, presents significant potential as a harmful light blocking material for various industrial applications, including automotive and architectural windows, optical lenses, and electronic screens like smartphones, tablets, and liquid–crystal displays.

As shown in the Fig. S3a, CCF-Fe3+1 has better UV and blue shielding performance than four commercially available harmful light shielding films. Fig. S3b compares the UV-C, UV-B, UV-A, and HEBL light blocking ratios of CCF-Fe3+1 with commercially available harmful light screening films. The blocking ratio of the four commercially available harmful light screening films for the UV-C region is 100 %, the blocking ratio for the UV-B and UV-A region is more than 93 %, and the blocking ratio for the HEBL region is the highest 72 %. The blocking ratio of CCF-Fe3+1 on both UV and HEBL regions reached 100 %, which is higher than the four commercially available harmful light screening films. The results show that the highly transparent CCF-Fe3+ films prepared in this study perform better in UV and HEBL barrier than the commercially available harmful light screening films. It can be seen that Fe3+ is an excellent UV and blue light absorber, and the synthesis method of CCF-Fe3+ film is simple and green, which is very suitable for commercial application.

Generally, full-color displays in electronic devices consist of blue (B), red (R), and green (G) LEDs [3]. In this context, CCF-Fe3+ film is expected to effectively screen harmful light while permitting the transmission of other wavelengths within the visible spectrum. Therefore, B, G, and R-LEDs were utilized to confirm the selective blue light screening performance of CCF-Fe3+ film. As illustrated in Fig. 5k, CCF-Fe3+ film exhibits differential screening properties for B, G, and R-LEDs. Notably, the green (G-LED) and red light (R-LED) remained virtually unchanged when shielded with CCF-Fe3+ film, indicating that CCF-Fe3+ film is highly selective in attenuating blue light while preserving the integrity of other light within the visible region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential applications: CCF-Fe3+ films as electronic screen protectors
ce:section.ce:para: As a practical application demonstration, we examined the capability of CCF-Fe3+ films to obstruct blue light emitted by lamplight, mobile screen, and computer phone screen. The blue light shielding ratio of various CCF-Fe3+ films was compared to that of commercially available blue light-screening glasses (G1 and G2) (Fig. S4). The blue light-blocking ratio of CCF for electronics is very low, suggesting that CCF cannot effectively filter blue light emitted from artificial lighting and electronics. With the increase of Fe3+ content, the blocking ratio of blue light progressively increased, and the shielding ratio of CCF-Fe3+ films for blue light exceeded that of G1 (85 %) and G2 (95 %). These results correspond with the variations in the intensity of blue, green, and orange light in the intensity spectra. The blue light-screening test is shown in Fig. 6 a. The intensity spectrum of blue light obstructed by CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films was evaluated. The capacity of films to screen blue light emitted by the lighting lamp was initially examined, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. Despite the existence of CCF, the blue light intensity is not significantly diminished, indicating that CCF cannot effectively screen blue light emitted by the lighting lamp. On the contrary, when CCF-Fe3+ films are utilized to screen blue light emitted by the lighting lamp, a significant reduction in the blue light intensity can be observed. This shows that the CCF-Fe3+ films can effectively screen the blue light emitted from the lamplight. As shown in Fig. 6b, CCF exhibits little screening effect on blue light emitted from mobile phone and desktop computer screens, as evidenced by the lack of significant change in blue light intensity from these devices after using CCF. When CCF-Fe3+ films were used to screen computer and mobile phone screens, almost all of harmful blue light can be blocked. Meanwhile, it can be observed from Fig. 6b that when CCF is used to block lamplight, computer screen and mobile phone screen, the intensity of several kinds of light does not change, which means that CCF has no light shielding and conversion abilities. When CCF-Fe3+ films are used to block lamplight, computer screen and mobile phone screen, the intensity of blue light significantly weakens or even disappears, while the intensity of green light increases, which also shows that blue light is converted into low-energy long-wavelength light, indicating that CCF-Fe3+ films have light shielding and conversion abilities. Meanwhile, we also tested the shielding performance of CCF-Fe3+ films on other electronic product (e.g., video game console) and lighting system (e.g., flashlight). As shown in Fig. S5, all the films can effectively block the blue light emitted by the screens.

The transparency and haze of the CCF-Fe3+ films were further analyzed. For some applications such as displays, clean windows, car windshields, and optoelectronic materials, CCF-Fe3+ films with low haze and high transmittance are urgently needed. Especially, when CCF-Fe3+ films are used in sunglasses or anti-blue light glasses, it is necessary that CCF-Fe3+ films have low haze. As depicted in Fig. 7 a-f, the building is distinctly visible through cellulose hydrogel, cellulose hydrogel-Fe3+, CCF, and CCF-Fe3+ films, revealing their high transparency and low haze, which significantly broadens their potential applications. Compared to other light-blocking films documented in the literature, CCF-Fe3+ films provide not only enhanced transparency but also reduced haze, alongside superior blue light attenuation. The transmittance values of CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films are shown in Fig. 7g. CCF-Fe3+ films exhibit superior light transmittance compared to numerous cellulose-derived materials and certain commercial polymers, such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET, <84.2 %) [45], but also endowed CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films with extremely low haze. As illustrated in Fig. 7g and 7 h, the CCF demonstrates not only exceptionally high transmittance (93.2 %) but also a minimal haze of merely 1.8 %. With the increase of Fe3+ content in the films, the transmittance of CCF-Fe3+ films diminishes while the haze augments. In contrast to traditional polymer films derived from petrochemicals, CCF-Fe3+ films possess lower haze, suggesting their potential to supplant these polymer films (Fig. 7i)[46]. These results all confirm that CCF-Fe3+ films with high transparency, low haze, and superior blue light absorption and conversion performance are applicable as anti-blue light films anti-blue light films for human protection against blue light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardancy properties of CCF-Fe3+ films
ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of flame-retardant transparent films is crucial for electronic devices and construction materials, yet remains a formidable challenge. Herein, flame-retardant cellulose-based films were synthesized from water-soluble metal salts. Unlike regular cellulose film-based products, the water-soluble metal salt used in the current study (FeCl3) are well known as non-combustible. To quantitative evaluate the flame-retardant capacity of CCF-Fe3+ films, MCC was utilized in this work, which has been proved to be a facile and efficacious test for assessing fire-resistance materials. Some flame retardancy parameters (e.g., peak heat release rate (PHRR), heat release capacity (HRC), total heat release (THR), and temperature at PHRR (T PHRR)) are depicted in Fig. 8 a to 8d, with comprehensive data summarized in Table S1. The incorporation of Fe3+ significantly enhances the barrier effect by facilitating the formation of an oxide layer post-combustion, thereby drastically decreasing the PHRR of CCF-Fe3+ films. More precisely, the PHRR for CCF-Fe3+1 was reduced from 203.8 W/g to 32.6 W/g. The PHRR of CCF-Fe3+2 decreased from 203.8 W/g to 24.1 W/g and that of CCF-Fe3+3 decreased from 203.8 W/g to 17.4 W/g with the increase in the content of Fe3+ in the films. Meanwhile, the HRC and THR of CCF-Fe3+ films were significantly reduced. The THR of CCF-Fe3+3 diminished to 1.6 MJ/m2, representing an 86.3 % reduction relative to CCF. Compared to the HRC value of CCF (236 J/K·g), CCF-Fe3+3 decreased remarkably to 21.5 J/K·g, manifesting that the introduction of Fe3+ reduced the flame hazard remarkably. Compared with CCF, the three parameters of the CCF-Fe3+ films are significantly reduced, providing unequivocal evidence that the incorporation of Fe3+ markedly augments the fire-resistance capacity of the films. All films demonstrated three stages of weight losswithin the temperature span of 30 ∼ 800°C. With the increase of Fe3+ content, the mass loss rate of CCF-Fe3+ films gradually decreased. These results manifest that the integration of Fe3+ effectively inhibited the thermal decomposition of cellulose, thereby markedly enhancing its fire-resistance capacity. The residue of CCF-Fe3+ films increased with rising of Fe3+ content. This is attributed to the thermal degradation of Fe3+ produced a large amount of Fe2O3 residue, which has high thermal stability. Hence, it is unsurprising that the total residues of composite films increased with the incorporation of more Fe3+. Conversely, the thermal degradation of CCF resulted in significantly less char residue. Such thermal stability can safeguard films from the effects of high temperature changes in practical applications.

An examination of flammability provides a more comprehensive and intuitive illustration of the fire-resistance properties inherent in CCF-Fe3+ films. Untreated cellulose filmis highly flammable. When exposed to a flame, CCF combusts rapidly and vigorously, leaving minimal ash (Fig. 8a and Video S3). Conversely,CCF-Fe3+ films exhibit immediate self-extinguishing behavior once the flame is removed (Videos S4-S6). The CCF-Fe3+ films demonstrated better self-extinguishing properties compared to CCF, with effectiveness dependent on ion concentration. CCF-Fe3+ films exhibited enhanced flame retardancy with increasing Fe3+ concentration. LOI testing is extensively employed to ascertain the flammability of polymeric materials. In this work, the LOI of CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films with varying Fe3+ content was measured, with results presented in Fig. 8h. CCF possess an LOI value of 19 %. With the introduction of Fe3+, the LOI of CCF-Fe3+ films increased from 19 % to 48 %, surpassing the industrial standard for flame retardant materials, which is 27 %. Based on the aforementioned discussion, the potential flame resistance mechanism of CCF-Fe3+ films is summarized in Fig. 8i. There are a large number of carboxyl groups on the surface of cellulose that can coordinate with Fe3+, which makes Fe3+ firmly adsorbed on the surface of cellulose. After the coordination of cellulose and Fe3+, the ionic layer is formed by stacking in the drying process. As shown in the videos, when CCF-Fe3+ films is ignited, a carbon layer containing Fe2O3 rapidly forms and CCF-Fe3+ films quickly extinguish. This suggested that the Fe3+ stacking layers potentially further dehydrated cellulose to char and suppressed the heat exothermic behavior. A Fe2O3 char layer formed, obstructing O2 access and heat propagation. Additionally, water molecules released during the reaction suppressed smoke and further enhanced flame retardancy. Meanwhile, when chlorine elements undergo thermal degradation, hydrogen chloride gas is released. Hydrogen chloride gas can react with highly reactive H· and O · H· free radicals in the flame, resulting in inert gas and Cl· free radicals. Compared with H· and OH· free radicals, the reactive activity of inert gas and Cl· free radicals is reduced a lot, so it has a lower ability to burn free radical oxidation, thereby reducing flammability. The improvement in fire-resistance may be due to the formation of hydrolysis products of trivalent cations. The heat absorption lowers the temperature; the water vapor dilutes the combustible gases; the oxide forms a barrier layer. The combined effect of these factors suppressed the combustion of the film [47].

The advantageous flexibility and mechanical integrity of CCF-Fe3+ films offer a distinct potential for the advancement of high-performance metal ion-based color conversion materials. The stress–strain profiles of CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films are depicted in Fig. S6a. The CCF displayed a tensile strength of 106.1 MPa, while a decline in tensile strength was observed for CCF-Fe3+ films correlating with increased Fe3+ content. The tensile strength of CCF-Fe3+1 was 94.8 MPa, which decreased by 10.6 %. The tensile strength of CCF-Fe3+3 is recorded at 87.9 MPa, representing a decline of 17.1 %. The tensile strength of CCF-Fe3+3 is 78.4 MPa, which decreases by 26.1 %. Yang et al. [25] synthesized UV-screening films by incorporating Fe3+ into TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibrils. The results show that the incorporation of Fe3+ significantly reduced the mechanical strength of the films (by 28.50 %), which may due to the disruption of hydrogen bond interactions, leading to a decrease in mechanical integrity. Although the incorporation of Fe3+ reduced the tensile strength of the composite films, but it remained above 78 MPa, surpassing the mechanical strength of most plastic films such as polycaprolactone (PCL), polybutylene succinate (PBS), polylactic acids (PLAs), polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs), poly (butylene adipate-co-terephthalate) (PBAT), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), high-density polyethylene (HDPE), polystyrene (PS), acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS),and utilized in optical material production (Fig. S6b)[48–55].

This cellulose-based light conversion material can be scaled up using a novel cellulose film production process (Fig. 9 a). A potential engineering production lines for light conversion film through a continuous roll-to-roll system is proposed (Fig. 9b). Fig. 9a demonstrates the photo of large size CCF-Fe3+ film. We propose that the three-step fabrication process for light conversion cellulose films (CCF-Fe3+) is scalable via a simple dipping method, including the carboxymethylation of cellulose fiber in sodium chloroacetate/sodium hydroxide solution via etherification reaction, the modified cellulose was dissolved in ionic liquid to prepare regenerated cellulose hydrogels, coordination between Fe3+ and cellulose molecules, and finally, washing and drying. Furthermore, the AmimCl (as a cellulose solvent) is recyclable, FeCl3 serves as the sole consumed raw material, and water is employed as the only solvent, rendering the process both cost-effective and sustainable. Altogether, this highly scalable, low-cost, and eco-friendly fabrication process endows the cellulose-based light conversion film with significant potential for practical applications. Fig. S7 demonstrates the light shielding and conversion performance of large-size CCF-Fe3+ film when indoor light is turned on and off. Table S2 is a comprehensive comparison of CCF-Fe3+1 with existing color conversion and light blocking materials [2–4,6,56–59]. Compared to these color conversion and light blocking films, CCF-Fe3+1 not only shows excellent UV and blue shielding performance, but also shows higher transparency. At present, most film materials with both color conversion and UV to blue light shielding are mainly composed of carbon dots with some substrates (such as PVA, CMC and hydroxypropyl methylcellulose). Carbon dot is a kind of material with the ability of light absorption and conversion, so it is often used as a light conversion material to prepare UV and blue light filtering films. These films composed of carbon dots and PVA show outstanding UV and blue light filtering capability, and the impact of this film on the environment is very small, because the raw material of these composite films is degradable. For example, although PVA is a petroleum-based material, it can degrade under certain conditions. CMC and hydroxypropyl methylcellulose are cellulose derivatives and can be biodegradable. At present, PVA film can be large-scale production, but for CMC film and hydroxypropyl methylcellulose film, the technology is not mature. In fact, the large-scale production of carbon dot-based UV and blue shielding films is greatly limited, mainly for several reasons: (1). There is no systematic, fast, simple and green high-purity carbon dot production strategy. The carbon dot purity of large-scale preparation is very low, generally less than 13 %, and most of the particle size is far greater than 10 nm. (2). Although dialysis is the most commonly used purification method for carbon dot, there are still no standards or guidelines for dialysis time and the interception molecular weight of dialysis membranes, and other less used purification methods have greater problems in this regard. Therefore, the current carbon dot-based UV and blue shielding film cannot be large-scale production. Compared with these reported light conversion and UV to blue light shielding films, our designed CCF-Fe3+ film has certain advantages in terms of environmental impact, optical performance, and large-scale production potential.

FTIR spectra of cellulose fibers, carboxymethylated cellulose fibers and CCF are presented in Fig. S8. The cellulose fibers display two primary peaks at 3336 and 2899 cm−1, attributed to − OH and asymmetric − CH stretching, respectively. Two peaks at 1644 and 1021 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C–O, respectively. The peak at 1590 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of COO–, proving the successful incorporation of carboxyl groups onto the cellulose skeleton [8]. The CCF exhibited two primary peaks at 3298 cm−1 and 2887 cm−1, with COO– appearing at 1590 cm−1. In the FTIR spectra of CCF-Fe3+ films, the chemical structure remained largely unchanged following Fe3+ adsorption onto the cellulose surface. The peak at 1633 cm−1 is attributed to the C=O stretching vibration of metal carboxylate groups.

The crystalline structure of CCF and CCF-Fe3+ films was characterized utilizing XRD. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the XRD pattern of CCF reveals three characteristic diffraction peaks at 12.4°, 20.2°, and 22.2°, corresponding to the (1–10), (110), and (020) planes of the cellulose Ⅱ crystal structure. Nevertheless, the existence of Fe3+ did not significantly alter the crystalline structure of cellulose film.

The internal structure of CCF-Fe3+ films was characterized using SEM. The cross-section SEM images reveal a tightly packed structure in the CCF-Fe3+ films (Figures. S10-S12). The adsorption of Fe3+ within the cellulose films was analyzed using EDS spectra. Moreover, the Fe3+ content in CCF-Fe3+ films were also determined via EDS analysis. The Fe3+ content of all film samples ranged from 3.06-4.25 % (Figures. S10-S12).

In summary, we found for the first time that a certain concentration of ferric chloride (FeCl3) solution can effectively shield UV and HEBL while converting short wavelength light (UV and blue light) to long wavelength light (yellow and green light). Inspired by FeCl3 solution, a biodegradable and sustainable cellulose film with integrated features, including low haze, high transparency, flame retardant, UV to blue light screening and color conversion performance, was simply prepared. Ultimately, the feasibility of employing CCF-Fe3+ films in practical WLED applications demonstrates their efficacy in obstructing harmful light. By means of light absorption and conversion, these films can proficiently attenuate blue light emanating from computers, mobile phone, video game console and illumination systems, along with UV radiation from solar exposure. By modulating the Fe3+ content within the films, we achieved cellulose films exhibiting varying degrees of blue light attenuation. Furthermore, the films containing Fe3+ blocked blue light better than commercially available blue light screening filters while maintaining high light transmittance (89.9–91.3 %) and low haze (2.0–2.1 %). Such film can be used as a color conversion layer, which is environmentally benign and effectively converts light. Simultaneously, the films show remarkable fire-resistance performance due to the existence of metal salt. Cellulose-based films offer significant advantages over synthetic polymer-based films, attributed to their nontoxicity, environmental friendliness, and biodegradability, which mitigate microplastic accumulation that poses growing threats to ecosystems and wildlife, alongside their cost-effectiveness. CCF-Fe3+ films possess substantial potential for concurrent light-blocking and wavelength down-conversion applications.

Our discoveries in this study provide a valuable proof of concept for the synthesis of cellulose-metal ion hybrid materials aimed at the development of practical films that counteract harmful blue light. Our findings achieve short-wave blue light conversion and may cause considerable repercussions to generate brand new envisages of cellulose-metal ion hybrid materials for the obstruction of harmful light. We intend to attempt more systems that can use our prepared light conversion film, such as windscreen and sunglasses in the near future.
